Theme 2: Reviewing, developing, and updating breast cancer screening guidelines (Recommendations 4, 6 and 8) Improving the processes to review, develop and update breast cancer screening guidelines is the focus of three recommendations in HESA’s Report and requires several considerations. The Government of Canada is addressing these recommendations through engagement and collaboration with relevant partners and stakeholders, the consideration of breast cancer detection and treatment technologies, mechanisms for periodic review of new evidence and even the consideration of socioeconomic factors that contribute to health inequities, including individual preferences and values. Corresponding to Recommendation 4, as directed by the former Minister of Health in his May 30, 2024 statement on breast cancer screening and prevention, Canada’s former Chief Public Health Officer engaged with key experts, including provincial and territorial officials from the Canadian Partnership Against Cancer (CPAC)’s Canadian Breast Cancer Screening Network (CBCSN), members of the Task Force and members of the United States Preventive Services Task Force to generate and share knowledge, lessons learned and best practices related to breast cancer screening. PHAC, in collaboration with key partners, also convened two stakeholder events related to breast cancer screening and prevention. On September 26 and 27, 2023, PHAC convened a twoday Knowledge Exchange Event to discuss the current state of the science, including knowledge gaps related to breast cancer screening. Planning for the event was supported by an Advisory Planning Committee (APC) consisting of representatives from Health Canada, the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR), CPAC, CPAC’s CBCSN, the Canadian Cancer Society, and the University of Toronto. Approximately 70 participants attended both days of the Event, including people with lived experience, Canadian and international experts, health professionals, academics and researchers, representatives from patient advocacy groups, Indigenous and racialized communities, non-governmental organizations and provincial and territorial officials. Topics covered during the Knowledge Exchange Event included domestic and international breast cancer screening guidelines; perspectives of patient and health care providers; and research and innovation opportunities for advancing breast cancer screening practices, such as shared decision-making and artificial intelligence. A “What We Heard” Report from the event was published online and shared with the Task Force to support the development of the draft updated Breast Cancer Screening Guideline. PHAC convened another event on February 26, 2025, with support from the APC, titled “Closing the Gap: Action for Equity in Breast Cancer Screening”. This event welcomed 57 participants and focused on equitable implementation of breast cancer screening services, including increasing awareness and educating partners and stakeholders on systemic and social barriers that prevent equitable access to breast cancer screening services for underserved groups; sharing knowledge and best practices that highlight evidence-based approaches, research findings and case studies of successful breast cancer screening programs and tools; and fostering collaboration through open dialogue. A resource that outlines key partners identified by participants, areas of potential action, best practices, lessons learned, and successful programs was shared with event participants and their organizations to help improve equitable implementation of breast cancer screening in Canada. In regard to Recommendation 6, PHAC has worked with the Task Force to develop a process to monitor emerging evidence for guideline topics to determine when updates are required. The Task Force’s process of monitoring evidence to update guidelines is outlined in their methods manual, which states that published guidelines undergo continuous surveillance via an evidence alert service, review of clinical trials and ongoing feedback from clinical experts and stakeholders to alert the Task

determine when updates are required. The Task Force’s process of monitoring evidence to update guidelines is outlined in their methods manual, which states that published guidelines undergo continuous surveillance via an evidence alert service, review of clinical trials and ongoing feedback from clinical experts and stakeholders to alert the Task Force of any significant new evidence that may lead to a decision to update a guideline earlier than anticipated. The threshold for “significant” new evidence varies by guideline, as strong recommendations based on multiple trials with high certainty data may require substantive new contrary evidence compared to guidelines with conditional recommendations and/or those with low certainty or limited data. As part of the scientific support that PHAC provides to the Task Force, PHAC also reviews evidence and prepares summaries for the Task Force on a monthly basis to determine if a guideline should be considered for early update or continue ongoing surveillance. Once a guideline is five years old, PHAC prepares an in-depth evidence report for the Task Force’s consideration to decide if a guideline topic should be updated, reaffirmed or sunset. One of the recommendations resulting from PHAC’s formal evaluation of the Task Force in 2022 was for PHAC to explore ways to improve the timeliness of the guideline development process. In response to this recommendation, PHAC worked with the Task Force to review, revise and streamline processes and roles; supported the development of a project management process; and identified and addressed common factors in the guideline development process that impede the timeliness of guideline publication. Lastly, the EER Panel assessed the rigour, agility, inclusiveness and timeliness of the scientific review process of the Task Force. The EER final report provides insights into opportunities to develop more frequent updates to guidelines that would be more responsive to the evolving scientific landscape. This includes the recommendation for PHAC to enable and support the Task Force to implement a phased approach to maintaining and updating high priority guidelines using living methods, including continuous evidence monitoring, the use of emerging technologies, and collaboration with international partners. As mentioned previously, PHAC is currently working with an IAP to review recommendations presented in the EER final report and inform the launch of a renewed Task Force in April 2026. The following initiatives highlight the Government of Canada’s commitment to addressing Recommendation 8, noting that full implementation of this recommendation requires ongoing collaboration with PTs. The Government of Canada plays an active convenor role in bringing together key experts, including provincial and territorial officials from the CBCSN, to generate and share knowledge, discuss lessons learned and share best practices related to breast cancer screening services, especially for women who are underserved or at higher risk for developing breast cancer. PHAC is working with Statistics Canada and CanPath to enhance the analysis of disaggregated breast cancer screening data by geographic location, age, race, ethnicity, stage at diagnosis, and participation in breast cancer screening mammography. Statistics Canada completed drafts of these articles in Spring 2025 with publications recently released in August 2025, and forthcoming. CanPath anticipates submitting its findings for publication in 2025. The findings of these analyses will be available to inform the potential development of recommendations for women who are underserved and at higher risk for developing breast cancer in future breast cancer screening guidelines. Further, Budget 2024 outlined the Government of Canada’s investment of close to $200 billion over 10 years, including $44 billion in new funding for PTs and Indigenous partners, to support the health care system. The Canada Health Transfer will increase by $2.6 billion in 2025-26 to bring it to a

Further, Budget 2024 outlined the Government of Canada’s investment of close to $200 billion over 10 years, including $44 billion in new funding for PTs and Indigenous partners, to support the health care system. The Canada Health Transfer will increase by $2.6 billion in 2025-26 to bring it to a total investment of $54.7 billion. This funding will be used by PTs to support overall health care for people in Canada, which may include further designing and implementing jurisdictional breast cancer screening programs for those at higher risk of developing breast cancer, should PTs choose to allocate funding to this priority health issue. Theme 3: Delivery of breast cancer screening services (Recommendations 12 and 13) The Government of Canada is committed to working with PTs, Indigenous partners and other relevant partners and stakeholders to strengthen Canada’s publicly funded health care system, including enhancing and expanding breast cancer screening programs and services and investing in health human resources. In regard to Recommendation 12, the Canada Health Act Diagnostic Services Policy (effective 2020) confirms the federal position that all medically necessary diagnostic services are insured services, regardless of the venue where the services are delivered (i.e., hospital or clinic). Health Canada continues to work with PTs to ensure that patients can access medically necessary diagnostic services free from barriers, including patient charges. Additionally, ISC’s Non-Insured Health Benefits Program provides medical transportation coverage, including travel, meals and accommodations to registered First Nations and recognized Inuit to access publicly funded screening services, such as breast cancer screening, when not available locally. The Government of Canada recognizes the importance of investing in health human resources, as outlined in Recommendation 13. In regard to this recommendation, Budget 2024 included the Government of Canada’s plan to provide close to $200 billion over 10 years, including $44.4 billion in new funding to PTs and Indigenous partners, to support the health care system. This investment included: • An immediate $2 billion top up to the Canada Health Transfer to address immediate pressures on the health care system, including health human resources; • A guarantee of five per cent annual growth for the Canada Health Transfer for five years (2023-24 to 2027-28), which is estimated to provide an additional $15.3 billion; • $25 billion over 10 years to advance shared health priorities through tailored bilateral agreements that will support the needs of people in each province and territory in four areas of shared priority: family health services; health workers and backlogs; mental health and substance use; and a modernized health system; • $2 billion over 10 years to address the unique challenges faced by Indigenous people related to fair and equitable access to quality and culturally safe health care services; and • $505 million over five years to the Canadian Institute for Health Information (CIHI), Canada Health Infoway and federal data partners to work with the PTs on developing new health data indicators, supporting the creation of Health Workforce Canada, advancing digital health tools, and enhancing efforts to use data to improve safety and quality of care. In addition, Budget 2021 announced an investment of $354 million over five years, beginning in 2021-22, to increase the number of nurses and other medical professionals in remote and isolated First Nations communities. To further highlight the Government of Canada’s successful efforts to invest in Health Human Resources, CIHI released a report titled, “Taking the pulse: Measuring shared priorities for Canadian health care” in October 2024. This report stated that almost all PTs have seen a net increase in the number of family physicians, nurse practitioners and nurses. Theme 4: Cancer research, data

Theme 4: Cancer research, data collection and modelling (Recommendations 7, 9 and 11) The Government of Canada is committed to supporting advancements in breast cancer prevention, detection and treatment and has demonstrated an active role in implementing Recommendation 7 through ongoing work between PHAC, CPAC and Statistics Canada. CPAC was established in 2007 by the Government of Canada to provide a pan-Canadian perspective to cancer control. CPAC receives $47.5 million in federal funding per year to accelerate the implementation of the Canadian Strategy for Cancer Control (Strategy). This Strategy aims to provide governments, non-government organizations and people in Canada access to research, information and decision-making tools to reduce the burden of cancer through coordinated and system-level practice and policy change. In line with the priorities of the Strategy, the OncoSim model was developed by Statistics Canada with leadership and support from CPAC. OncoSim is a free, web-based simulation tool that evaluates cancer control strategies. OncoSim is calibrated to simulate the Canadian population according to observed and projected demographic characteristics by combining data from the real world, expert opinion and published literature to project health and economic outcomes. These outcomes are then attributed to 19 cancer risk factors, such as smoking and physical inactivity. It currently models four cancer sites (breast, colorectal, lung and cervical) and related screening programs in detail and provides high-level projections for 25 cancer sites. This unique tool is used by decision-makers across Canada, including the Task Force, to better understand the impact and value of cancer control interventions and develop cancer screening guidelines. CPAC and Statistics Canada are undertaking a project to improve the performance of the OncoSim-Breast microsimulation model by refining its inputs and projections related to breast density. Updating and refining the OncoSim-Breast microsimulation model will ensure that the most accurate and relevant outputs are produced and used when developing future breast cancer screening guidelines. The Government of Canada is also undertaking initiatives that are in support of Recommendation 9. PHAC is supporting Statistics Canada with four projects launched in 20242025 to enhance the analysis of ongoing population-level assessments and disaggregate data by race and ethnicity. These projects explore insights from the Canadian Cancer Registry, the Canadian Census Health and Environment Cohorts and the Canadian Community Health Survey: breast cancer outcomes by race and ethnicity; temporal trends of breast cancer by race and ethnicity; participation in breast cancer screening mammography; and geographic variation in breast cancer incidence, stage at diagnosis and survival. As previously noted, Statistics Canada completed drafts of these articles in Spring 2025 that highlight the results of their analyses, with publications recently released in August 2025, and forthcoming, including one specifically examining breast cancer characteristics and outcomes in Canadian Black women by ancestry. To date, Statistics Canada has enhanced the data linkage that allows for the identification of race and ethnicity information for breast cancer cases in the Canadian Cancer Registry and breast cancer deaths in the Canadian Vital Statistics Deaths Database. The availability of this information has been improved with better coverage of the population, as it now includes more cases from Quebec as well as more recent years of data available (up to 2021 for cancer and up to 2023 for deaths). With support from PHAC and Women and Gender Equality Canada in 2023-2024, Statistics Canada also conducted and published five studies on breast

as it now includes more cases from Quebec as well as more recent years of data available (up to 2021 for cancer and up to 2023 for deaths). With support from PHAC and Women and Gender Equality Canada in 2023-2024, Statistics Canada also conducted and published five studies on breast cancer that explored incidence trends in younger women; impact of organized screening programs on stage at diagnosis in younger women; impact of breast cancer screening on 10-year net survival in younger women; molecular and histological subtypes of breast cancer in Canada; and breast cancer incidence and mortality by age, stage, and molecular subtypes by race/ethnicity in Canada. The purpose of these studies was to provide insight into risk factors for early onset breast cancer for women in Canada. As mentioned previously, PHAC collaborates with Statistics Canada and the Canadian Cancer Society to produce regular biennial cancer surveillance reports, with alternating brief summary reports published in the Canadian Medical Association Journal. These reports are the most upto-date statistics on breast cancer incidence, mortality and survival over time, while special topic reports may include information on breast cancer stage at diagnosis and the cost of breast cancer on the health care system, patients and caregivers. In Fall 2024, PHAC provided $295,000 to CanPath to examine differences in breast cancer screening participation, cancer treatments and outcomes by race and ethnicity using existing Canadian cohort and administrative data. Lastly, the Government of Canada is supportive of Indigenous-led data collection efforts and data sovereignty in this area. This includes the work of the First Nations Information Governance Centre (FNIGC). The FNIGC is an independent, non-profit organization that collects relevant information related to First Nations living on reserve and analyzes and manages the health data they collect in alignment with First Nations data sovereignty principles. One questionnaire funded by the Government of Canada and administered to First Nations adults aged 18 and over included questions about health conditions, including breast cancer. These questions included the age at diagnosis and whether the respondent has undergone treatment. In regard to Recommendation 11, the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) has invested over $1.1 billion in priority-driven and investigator-led cancer research between 20192020 and 2023-2024, including through its dedicated Institute of Cancer Research. Approximately $147 million of this total supported research related to breast cancer, such as research that aims to create a rapid evaluation framework to help PTs quickly assess and update cancer screening guidelines; identify barriers to cancer screening participation and improve screening uptake; and to better identify individuals at higher risk of breast cancer to guide earlier and fairer screening. In 2024, CIHR’s Institute of Cancer Research also launched the Team Grants: Bringing Biology to Cancer Prevention funding opportunity, which includes a dedicated Breast Cancer Pool to advance prevention and early detection of breast cancer. CIHR led the development of a roadmap that summarized research gaps relating to breast cancer screening in consultation with federal partners, oncologists, health organizations, researchers and people with lived and living experience in 2024. The research roadmap identified gaps in two key areas: social determinants of health and technology strategies. Further research is needed regarding breast cancer screening practices in diverse populations and the impact of information and personal values on individual decision-making about testing. Likewise, more data is required to better understand the relationship between different screening technologies and health outcomes, as well as the efficiency of various screening strategies at a population level. CIHR’s research roadmap was shared with key experts, federal partners and stakeholders, and will be used to inform future research priorities and potential investments in this

Theme 5: Public education and awareness raising of breast cancer screening (Recommendation 10) The Government of Canada recognizes the importance of ensuring that accurate, evidencebased and accessible information on breast cancer screening and prevention is available for all people in Canada. The Government of Canada also recognizes the importance of public health education to reduce knowledge barriers. Many current initiatives are supportive of Recommendation 10, as they support public education and awareness raising to ensure that diverse groups of women can access and make informed decisions about their breast health. Ministerial messages and social media content were publicly shared for Breast Cancer Awareness Month in October 2024 and 2025. These messages highlighted the importance of breast cancer awareness and early detection and reiterated that the Government of Canada is committed to advancing women’s health through comprehensive breast cancer screening and prevention efforts. PHAC continues to develop and implement ongoing social media campaigns that highlight the importance of cancer screening, including breast cancer. Social media content was created for National Cancer Wellness Awareness Day in June 2024, and Cancer Awareness Month in September 2024 and 2025. The Government of Canada also continues to inform the public about new initiatives and investments related to breast cancer screening and prevention. For example, on November 29, 2024, PHAC issued a news release and supporting social media content, to announce an investment of $545,000 in breast cancer screening and research for CanPath and the Canadian Cancer Society. CanPath received $295,000 for a project that aims to generate better disaggregated data to inform best cancer screening practices in Canada. The Canadian Cancer Society received $250,000 to scale-up their successful Memo-mamo public awareness and social media campaign in Quebec to reach women across Canada, especially those at higher risk of developing breast cancer who have never been screened or have not been getting screened regularly. The Canadian Cancer Society’s awareness campaign ran from February 10, 2025, to March 31, 2025, and included a new webpage providing information on getting a mammogram in Canada. The campaign was positively received, with the majority of respondents to the campaign evaluation finding it effective in encouraging mammogram uptake, seeking information and discussing screening with healthcare providers. The campaign evaluation also identified several barriers to breast cancer screening, including lack of a medical recommendation, unawareness of age-based guidelines and lack of access to a family doctor. Additional concerns included fear of pain, cost misconceptions and uncertainty about how to access breast cancer screening. The strongest motivators for breast cancer screening were a doctor’s recommendation, personalized reminders, and mobile clinic availability. Women aged 40–49, those without a family doctor, and residents of Quebec were highlighted as priority groups for targeted outreach due to lower awareness, engagement and intention to be screened. PHAC also issued a news release and supporting social media content on February 26, 2025, to highlight progress on breast cancer screening research and implementation efforts in Canada. The update spoke to the Government of Canada’s commitment to supporting the health of women by addressing knowledge gaps in breast cancer screening and prevention. Additionally, PHAC commissioned public opinion research (POR) on the barriers to equitably accessing breast cancer screening services between January and February 2025. The POR, administered to 1,800 Canadian women, aged 40 and older, has been useful in gaining

of women by addressing knowledge gaps in breast cancer screening and prevention. Additionally, PHAC commissioned public opinion research (POR) on the barriers to equitably accessing breast cancer screening services between January and February 2025. The POR, administered to 1,800 Canadian women, aged 40 and older, has been useful in gaining a better understanding of awareness, participation and perceived barriers to breast cancer screening across different breast cancer screening programs and population subgroups, including Indigenous, Black, racialized and newcomer women. Findings from the POR indicate that awareness of routine breast cancer screening programs is relatively high, with 76% of women reporting familiarity with services available in their area. Similarly, 71% of women self-reported awareness of the screening recommendations applicable to women aged 40 and older who are not at increased risk. However, only 60% of women feel well-informed about the associated risk factors, highlighting an ongoing need for public education and engagement. The results from this POR were shared with stakeholders to help inform potential future policy and programmatic work and are publicly available on the Government of Canada’s Publications database. Conclusion The Government of Canada is committed to improving the health and well-being of all people in Canada. As noted in this Government Response, the Government of Canada agrees with the committee’s recommendations, and continues to demonstrate leadership to address issues related to breast cancer screening and prevention by actively engaging and investing in initiatives with PTs and other key non-governmental partners and stakeholders. This work has also been catalyzed across the federal government by the former Minister of Health’s statement on breast cancer screening and prevention in May 2024. Many of these initiatives are addressing and responding to the unique needs of women in Canada, with an emphasis on improving breast cancer screening to respond to the realities of Canada’s diverse population and how women in Canada interact with the health care system. The approach taken by the Government of Canada allows it to create tailored solutions that are not only inclusive but also respond to the various experiences faced by women from diverse backgrounds. The Government of Canada’s demonstrated commitment to advancing women’s health reflects a holistic and innovative approach to achieving better outcomes in breast cancer screening, while empowering women in Canada to make informed decisions about their health supported by the best available science and evidence.
